title: Character Rigidity and Ergodic Actions of Non-uniform Higher Rank Lattices
speaker: Alon Dogon
abstract:
The theory of rigidity for lattices in higher rank semisimple Lie groups is a powerful and exciting subject, combining methods from algebra, number theory, geometry and dynamics. One of the most celebrated results is Margulis' normal subgroup theorem, which states that any normal subgroup of a higher rank lattice is either central or of finite index. Conjectures due to Connes, as well as Stuck-Zimmer, suggest significant strengthenings of this statement in the context of ergodic theory and representation theory of higher rank lattices. Connes' conjecture states that any extremal, positive definite, normalised central function (also known as a character) defined on a lattice is either the trace of a finite dimensional representation, or vanishes off the center.
I will present a recent joint work with Glasner, Gorfine, Hanany and Levit which proves this conjecture for non-uniform lattices in products of rank-1 groups over arbitrary local fields of uneven characteristic, as well as Lie groups with infinite center. Together with previous results of other authors, this fully establishes Connes and Stuck-Zimmer's conjectures for all non-uniform lattices.

proof_stages.label: Higher-rank arithmetic lattice setting
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes concrete S-arithmetic examples, explains their realization as lattices in products of Lie and p-adic groups, and isolates the higher-rank condition together with the property-T versus property-tau distinction.

proof_stages.label: Margulis normal subgroup rigidity paradigm
proof_stages.summary: Margulis' normal subgroup theorem is stated for irreducible higher-rank lattices, and its proof philosophy is summarized as showing that a noncentral quotient is simultaneously property T and amenable, hence finite.

proof_stages.label: Stuck-Zimmer and invariant random subgroups
proof_stages.summary: The normal-subgroup theorem is upgraded to a probabilistic/ergodic statement: under property T, ergodic probability-measure-preserving actions are either essentially free or finite, equivalently ergodic invariant random subgroups are trivial/central or finite-index.

proof_stages.label: Traces and characters of discrete groups
proof_stages.summary: The representation-theoretic object of study is introduced: a trace is a normalized positive-definite conjugation-invariant function, equivalently a tracial von Neumann algebra representation, and characters are the extreme points of the compact convex trace simplex.

proof_stages.label: Character rigidity conjectures and known cases
proof_stages.summary: After contrasting with the wild trace theory of free groups, the talk states Connes' character-rigidity conjecture for higher-rank lattices, the Stuck-Zimmer conjecture without property T, and prior results proving character rigidity when a property-T factor is present or in special SL_2-type S-arithmetic cases.

proof_stages.label: Main nonuniform character-rigidity theorem
proof_stages.summary: The joint theorem is stated: if Gamma is a higher-rank lattice and either the ambient group has a property-T factor or the lattice is nonuniform, then Gamma is character rigid; the discussion also clarifies its relation to property tau and IRS rigidity.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic and diagonal-dynamics proof strategy
proof_stages.summary: For the new nonuniform rank-one-product cases, the proof uses Margulis arithmeticity to pass to arithmetic groups, exploits infinite-order diagonal elements coming from units, and then proves vanishing of non-finite-dimensional characters on larger and larger algebraic pieces by conjugation dynamics.

proof_stages.label: Limitations and approximation consequences
proof_stages.summary: The speaker identifies cocompact lattices in products of rank-one groups as the main remaining open case and answers questions clarifying nonuniformity and the finite-dimensional approximation question, where character rigidity reduces the issue to approximation of the regular trace via residual finiteness.

examples.name: Free groups and the Poulsen trace simplex
examples.note: The free group F_n is used as the wild opposite of character rigidity: its trace simplex is Poulsen and not all traces are approximable by finite-dimensional traces.
examples.engagement_reason: Beyond citing the Poulsen simplex theorem for free groups, the speaker works through the usual trace example using left and right regular representations and the invariant delta vector.
